title: Animal Hierarchy
description:
Build a simple animal kingdom using inheritance!

Create an Animal superclass and two subclasses: Dog and Cat. Each animal has a name, and each type of animal speaks differently.

Your Animal class should have:
- A protected String field called `name`
- A constructor that takes a name
- A `speak()` method that returns `"..."`

Your Dog class should extend Animal and override `speak()` to return `"Woof! My name is "` followed by the name.

Your Cat class should extend Animal and override `speak()` to return `"Meow! My name is "` followed by the name.

Create a Dog named "Buddy" and a Cat named "Whiskers", then print what each one says.
expectedOutput:
Woof! My name is Buddy
Meow! My name is Whiskers
hints: Start by writing the Animal class with a protected String name field and a constructor: public Animal(String name) { this.name = name; }
For each subclass, the constructor should call super(name) to pass the name up to Animal. Then use @Override on the speak() method.
In main: Dog buddy = new Dog("Buddy"); then System.out.println(buddy.speak());

title: Shape Hierarchy
description:
Create a shape calculator using inheritance!

Build a Shape superclass with a method `area()` that returns 0.0 by default. Then create two subclasses:

**Circle** — has a `radius` field. Its `area()` should return π × radius² (use `Math.PI`).

**Rectangle** — has `width` and `height` fields. Its `area()` should return width × height.

In main, create a Circle with radius 5 and a Rectangle with width 4 and height 6. Print each shape's area with a label.
hints: The Shape class is simple — just one method: public double area() { return 0.0; }
In Circle, store the radius in the constructor, then override area() with: return Math.PI * radius * radius;
In main: Circle c = new Circle(5); System.out.println("Circle area: " + c.area());

title: Vehicle Hierarchy
description:
Build a vehicle dealership system using inheritance with super() calls and method overriding!

Create a **Vehicle** superclass with:
- Protected fields: `make` (String) and `year` (int)
- A constructor that takes make and year
- A `describe()` method that returns the year and make (e.g., "2023 Toyota")
- A `honk()` method that returns "Beep!"

Create a **Car** subclass with:
- A private `numDoors` field (int)
- A constructor that takes make, year, and numDoors — call super() first!
- Override `describe()` to add door info using super.describe() (e.g., "2023 Toyota (4-door car)")
- Override `honk()` to return "Honk honk!"

Create a **Truck** subclass with:
- A private `payloadTons` field (double)
- A constructor that takes make, year, and payloadTons — call super() first!
- Override `describe()` to add payload info using super.describe() (e.g., "2022 Ford (truck, 2.5T payload)")
- Override `honk()` to return "HOOOONK!"

Create a Car ("Toyota", 2023, 4) and a Truck ("Ford", 2022, 2.5). Print each vehicle's description and honk.
expectedOutput:
2023 Toyota (4-door car)
Honk honk!
2022 Ford (truck, 2.5T payload)
HOOOONK!
hints: Vehicle constructor: public Vehicle(String make, int year) { this.make = make; this.year = year; } and describe() returns year + " " + make
In Car's constructor, the FIRST line must be super(make, year); — then set this.numDoors = numDoors. Override describe() with: return super.describe() + " (" + numDoors + "-door car)";
The Truck class follows the same pattern as Car. Its describe() should return: super.describe() + " (truck, " + payloadTons + "T payload)"

title: Employee & Manager
description:
Build a payroll system that demonstrates polymorphism with arrays!

Create an **Employee** class with:
- Protected fields: `name` (String) and `salary` (double)
- A constructor that takes name and salary
- A `getTotalCompensation()` method that returns salary
- Override `toString()` to return the name and salary (e.g., "Alice | Salary: $50000.0")

Create a **Manager** class that extends Employee with:
- A private `bonus` field (double)
- A constructor that takes name, salary, and bonus — call super() first!
- Override `getTotalCompensation()` to return salary + bonus
- Override `toString()` to also show the bonus (e.g., "Bob | Salary: $70000.0 | Bonus: $15000.0")

In main, create a polymorphic array of type `Employee[]` containing:
1. new Employee("Alice", 50000)
2. new Manager("Bob", 70000, 15000)
3. new Employee("Charlie", 45000)
4. new Manager("Diana", 80000, 20000)

Loop through the array and print each person's info and total compensation.
expectedOutput:
Alice | Salary: $50000.0 => Total: $50000.0
Bob | Salary: $70000.0 | Bonus: $15000.0 => Total: $85000.0
Charlie | Salary: $45000.0 => Total: $45000.0
Diana | Salary: $80000.0 | Bonus: $20000.0 => Total: $100000.0
hints: Employee's toString(): return name + " | Salary: $" + salary; and Manager's toString(): return name + " | Salary: $" + salary + " | Bonus: $" + bonus;
The key to polymorphism: declare the array as Employee[] but put Manager objects in it too. Java will call the correct overridden methods at runtime!
In the loop: for (Employee e : staff) { System.out.println(e + " => Total: $" + e.getTotalCompensation()); } — Java automatically calls toString() when you use an object in string concatenation.

title: Game Character Hierarchy
description:
Build an RPG party system with inheritance and polymorphism! 🎮

Create a **GameCharacter** superclass with:
- Protected fields: `name` (String) and `health` (int)
- A constructor that takes name and health
- An `attack()` method that returns the name + " does a basic attack!"
- A `specialAbility()` method that returns the name + " has no special ability."
- Override `toString()` to return the name and HP (e.g., "Thor [HP: 120]")

Create three subclasses:

**Warrior** — has a private `armor` (int) field.
- Override `attack()`: return name + " swings a mighty sword! (Armor: " + armor + ")"
- Override `specialAbility()`: return name + " raises shield! Defense boosted!"

**Mage** — has a private `mana` (int) field.
- Override `attack()`: return name + " casts a fireball! (Mana: " + mana + ")"
- Override `specialAbility()`: return name + " teleports away! Mana remaining: " + (mana - 20)

**Archer** — has a private `arrows` (int) field.
- Override `attack()`: return name + " shoots an arrow! (Arrows left: " + (arrows - 1) + ")"
- Override `specialAbility()`: return name + " fires a volley of 3 arrows! (Arrows left: " + (arrows - 3) + ")"

Create a polymorphic `GameCharacter[]` party with:
- new Warrior("Thor", 120, 50)
- new Mage("Gandalf", 80, 100)
- new Archer("Legolas", 90, 30)

Print all party members, then all attacks, then all special abilities (with headers).
expectedOutput:
=== Party Members ===
Thor [HP: 120]
Gandalf [HP: 80]
Legolas [HP: 90]

=== Attack Phase ===
Thor swings a mighty sword! (Armor: 50)
Gandalf casts a fireball! (Mana: 100)
Legolas shoots an arrow! (Arrows left: 29)

=== Special Abilities ===
Thor raises shield! Defense boosted!
Gandalf teleports away! Mana remaining: 80
Legolas fires a volley of 3 arrows! (Arrows left: 27)
hints: Start with GameCharacter: protected fields, constructor that sets them, and default attack()/specialAbility() methods. Don't forget to @Override toString()!
Each subclass constructor must call super(name, health) FIRST, then set its own field (armor, mana, or arrows). Use @Override on attack() and specialAbility().

In main, use GameCharacter[] party = { new Warrior(...), new Mage(...), new Archer(...) }; Then use three separate for-each loops with System.out.println() headers between them. Use System.out.println() with no arguments for blank lines.
